title: Nutrition Without the Noise
subtitle: What we actually know, and what we just argue about
description: A full-length conversational course that cuts through the contradictions of popular nutrition advice and explains what the evidence actually supports.
targetAudience: Adults tired of contradictory headlines about food
objectives: Tell the difference between evidence and opinion in nutrition
Build a sustainable way of eating that isn't a fad
Spot the red flags of pseudoscience wellness content

modules.title: Why Is This So Confusing?
modules.description: Before we talk about food, let's talk about why the advice keeps changing.
objectives: Understand what nutrition science can and can't prove

lessons.title: Nutrition Is a Hard Science
lessons.description: And not in the way you think.
lessons.content: You can't lock people in a lab for 30 years and control what they eat. So nutrition studies mostly rely on people remembering what they ate last week, which is about as accurate as you'd expect. That's why so much advice wobbles.
keyPoints: Most nutrition studies are observational, not experimental
Asking 'what did you eat last year?' is a terrible data source
The loudest claims usually have the weakest evidence

lessons.title: How a Headline Is Born
lessons.description: The six-step journey from a small study to your phone.
lessons.content: A modest finding gets amplified by a press release, rewritten by a journalist, simplified by an editor, clickified by a headline writer, and finally distorted by every social post about it. By the time it reaches you, 'some association in mice' has become 'red wine cures cancer.'
keyPoints: University press offices reward dramatic framings
Journalists rarely read the actual paper
If a claim sounds like a miracle, something was lost in translation

lessons.title: The Real Rules
lessons.description: What the evidence actually keeps pointing at.
lessons.content: Strip out the trendy claims and you're left with a pretty boring list: eat more plants, less ultra-processed food, less sugar-sweetened drinks, and about the right number of calories for you. That's about 80% of good nutrition advice, and it's been stable for decades.
keyPoints: Boring advice is the advice with the strongest evidence
'Eat food, not too much, mostly plants' holds up well
Exciting-sounding exceptions usually don't replicate

modules.title: Macronutrients, Calmly
modules.description: What they are and why the arguments about them are mostly oversold.
objectives: Know what proteins, carbs, and fats actually do
Understand why diets can 'work' for opposite reasons

lessons.title: Protein Isn't Magic (But It's Important)
lessons.description: How much you need and where to get it.
lessons.content: Protein is what your body builds itself from. You probably need about 0.8 to 1.2 grams per kilogram of body weight daily — more if you're very active or older. Most people in wealthy countries easily hit that without trying.
keyPoints: Animal protein is efficient but optional
Plant proteins combined across a day cover all amino acids
Protein shakes are convenient, not required

lessons.title: Carbs Aren't the Enemy
lessons.description: The difference between an apple and a candy bar.
lessons.content: 'Carbs' covers everything from lettuce to lollipops, and treating them as one category is where confusion starts. Whole foods with fibre act totally differently in your body than refined sugar.
keyPoints: Fibre changes how quickly carbs hit your bloodstream
The glycemic index is a rough guide, not a rule
Cutting all carbs is neither required nor sustainable for most people

lessons.title: Fat Made a Comeback
lessons.description: Why the low-fat craze aged badly.
lessons.content: From the 1980s to the 2000s, fat was the villain. Then the food industry replaced fat with sugar, people got fatter anyway, and the story fell apart. Fat isn't a villain — it's just calorie-dense.
keyPoints: Saturated fat is still worth moderating
Unsaturated fats have decent cardiovascular evidence
Fat makes food taste good, which is a feature not a bug

lessons.title: Why Every Diet 'Works'
lessons.description: The dirty secret behind diet bestsellers.
lessons.content: Low-carb, Mediterranean, intermittent fasting, vegan — they all work for some people. What they share is getting you to eat fewer calories, usually by cutting out ultra-processed food. The specific mechanism is mostly marketing.
keyPoints: Calories matter; you can't cheat thermodynamics
Different diets make calorie reduction easier for different people
The best diet is one you can live with, honestly

modules.title: Eating Like a Human
objectives: Build a flexible default eating pattern

lessons.title: The Pantry Test
lessons.description: What's in your kitchen determines what you eat.
lessons.content: If your pantry is stocked with fruit, nuts, beans, and grains, you'll eat those. If it's stocked with chips and cookies, you'll eat those. The battle is won at the grocery store, not at the dinner table.
keyPoints: Don't rely on willpower at 10pm — shop well at 10am
Keep 'default snacks' that are actually good
Ban 'always' lists — just make better things more available

lessons.title: Plating Logic
lessons.description: A quarter-plate method that beats calorie counting.
lessons.content: Half your plate: vegetables. A quarter: protein. A quarter: starch. Add a bit of fat for flavour. It's a heuristic, not a rule — but it works for most meals and you never need to weigh anything.
keyPoints: Half a plate of veg gets most people more fibre than they'd otherwise eat
The protein quarter keeps you full
The starch is there for energy and because starch is nice

lessons.title: Eating Out
lessons.description: Because you're going to, and that's fine.
lessons.content: Restaurant food is often calorie-dense because fat and salt sell. The defence isn't to refuse — it's to plan: look at the menu before you're hangry, share dishes, drink water between glasses of wine.
keyPoints: You can be 80% sensible and 20% flexible and still be healthy
Social eating has benefits the kale doesn't
One restaurant meal won't derail anything

lessons.title: Hunger Is a Signal, Not an Emergency
lessons.description: Relearning what 'a bit hungry' feels like.
lessons.content: If you eat every two hours, you never actually get hungry — which is fine, but it means you don't notice when you are. Most people can comfortably go 4-5 hours between meals, and the sky doesn't fall.
keyPoints: Distinguish hunger from habit, thirst, and boredom
A bit of hunger isn't dangerous — it's information
Pre-planned meal timing beats grazing for most people

modules.title: Red Flags
modules.description: How to spot a nutrition scam in 30 seconds.

lessons.title: The Scam Vocabulary
lessons.description: Words that should make you suspicious.
lessons.content: 'Detox.' 'Cleanse.' 'Boost your metabolism.' 'Superfood.' These terms are unregulated and mostly meaningless. If someone uses three of them in a row, close the tab.
keyPoints: Your liver already detoxes for you — free
No food is 'super' in any meaningful way
'Boost your metabolism' is not a thing you can really do

lessons.title: The Supplement Question
lessons.content: Most people on varied diets don't need supplements. Exceptions: vitamin D if you don't see sun, B12 if you're vegan, iron if your blood work shows deficiency. Beyond those, the evidence gets thin fast.
keyPoints: Multivitamins don't help healthy people live longer
Food beats pills for most nutrients we've measured
Blood work first, supplements second — not the other way around

lessons.title: When to See a Real Professional
lessons.content: 'Registered Dietitian' is a regulated title with years of training behind it. 'Nutritionist' is, in most places, unregulated — anyone can call themselves one. If you have a real medical condition, go to the regulated ones.
keyPoints: Regulation matters — check titles and licensing in your country
A good dietitian asks questions; a bad one sells a product
An Instagram following is not a clinical qualification

quiz.question: What's the most common reason different diets can all 'work' for weight loss?
options: They each activate a special metabolic switch
They all lead participants to eat fewer calories in practice
They all eliminate specific 'toxins' from the body
quiz.explanation: The common thread is a reduction in total calorie intake, usually by cutting ultra-processed food — the specific macronutrient story is mostly branding.
